description: The GPIO peripheral exposes up to 32 signals arranged as two banks of 16 pins. Control and data registers are organized per pair of banks, with bit positions mapping directly to pin numbers. This banking simplifies bulk enable and status operations. Register naming follows the XY convention where X and Y denote the two banks. The table maps each GP[n] to its bank and bit index.
evidence: Section 2.2 Signal Descriptions; Section 2.5 GPIO Register Structure; Table 1
Remarks: Plan pin usage across banks for efficient ISR demultiplexing and EDMA triggers.

description: Each pin can be configured as input (=1) or output (=0) via DIR01, with all pins defaulting to inputs after reset. Direction is independently selectable per pin across both banks. Software must program DIR before driving outputs. Reads of DIR reflect configuration state. This is the primary mode control for GPIO pins.
Remarks: Initialize DIR only after pinmux assigns the pad to GPIO to avoid contention.

description: Outputs can be driven using either a single OUT_DATA01 read-modify-write or atomic set/clear via SET_DATA01 and CLR_DATA01. SET drives selected outputs high and CLR drives them low without affecting other bits. OUT_DATA mirrors the programmed output states, not the actual pad level if configured as input. This provides safe multi-context control without critical sections.
Remarks: Prefer SET/CLR for ISR-safe, lockless bit updates across software contexts.

description: IN_DATA01 returns the synchronized state of each GPIO pin. For pins configured as inputs, it reflects the debounced state aligned to the GPIO clock domain. For pins configured as outputs, it returns the value being driven (or wired-logic value for open-drain pads). This allows software to verify external levels and output assertions.
Remarks: When using open-drain or wired-OR connections, rely on IN_DATA to read the resolved bus state.

description: Each GPIO pin can generate an interrupt on rising, falling, both, or no edges. Edge detection is synchronized to the GPIO peripheral clock to avoid metastability. Control is via SET_RIS_TRIG/CLR_RIS_TRIG and SET_FAL_TRIG/CLR_FAL_TRIG bitmaps. This enables flexible event signaling from external pins. Direction does not need to be input for interrupt generation, useful for debug.
Remarks: Configure only one edge by setting SET on the desired edge and CLR on the other to prevent double interrupts.

description: Bank-level enable in BINTEN controls whether pins in bank 0 or 1 can act as interrupt sources. INTSTAT01 provides per-pin pending status with write-1-to-clear semantics. Software must demultiplex pending bits and clear them after service. No GPIO interrupts are multiplexed with other functions on this device. This simplifies ISR design and latency control.
evidence: Section 2.10.2; Section 2.10.4–2.10.5; Section 3.2 BINTEN; Section 3.12 INTSTAT01
Remarks: Use read-mask-clear (write-1-to-clear) on INTSTAT01 to avoid spurious clears on unrelated pins.

feature_name: EDMA event generation from GPIO edges
description: GPIO edge events can be routed as synchronization events to the EDMA. Bank enable bits in BINTEN affect both interrupt and EDMA event routing. This supports DMA-driven capture or processing pipelines triggered by external signals. Event details are device-specific and described in the data manual. Integration offloads the CPU for high-rate edge activity.
evidence: Section 1.2 Features; Section 2.10.1; Section 2.11 EDMA Event Support; Section 3.2 BINTEN note
dependencies: EDMA controller; BINTEN; CPU/SoC event crossbar (device DM)
Remarks: Align EDMA PaRAM options to edge frequency; ensure ISR and EDMA do not race on shared flags.

description: The GPIO peripheral clock is disabled by default and must be enabled via the local PSC. Once enabled, the GPIO clock runs at CPU/6. Edge detection and input synchronization operate in this clock domain. Correct clocking is prerequisite for register access and interrupt operation. Software must manage clock gating around low-power modes.
Remarks: Enable the PSC module before any GPIO register access; otherwise reads return reset values and writes are ineffective.

description: The PSC controls GPIO power states, enabling reduced-power modes during inactivity. While in low-power, interrupt generation is suspended until the peripheral is re-enabled. Output pins retain their driven states across low-power entry. Software must sequence PSC transitions to avoid missing critical edges.
Remarks: Before entering low-power, quiesce edge sources or mask BINTEN to prevent lost events.

description: GPIO shares package pins with other peripherals via extensive pin multiplexing. Pinmux is configured by a combination of reset configuration and software-programmable registers described in the device data manual. GPIO functionality is available only when the pad is assigned to GPIO. This allows flexible board-level pin reuse. Incorrect pinmux can prevent GPIO operation.
Remarks: Always apply pinmux before DIR/OUT configuration to avoid bus contention with other peripherals.

description: GPIO state reacts differently to reset sources. A generic emulator-initiated software reset does not modify GPIO configuration or pin states. A PSC-driven reset sequence (clock disable, PSC reset, clock enable) restores default register settings. A hardware reset returns configuration and data registers to reset defaults, affecting pin direction and output states.
Remarks: After hardware or PSC reset, re-run pinmux and initialization to reestablish intended IO states.

description: After hardware reset, software must configure pin multiplexing, enable the GPIO module via PSC, and program direction/data/interrupt registers. This three-step sequence ensures pins are correctly assigned, clocked, and functional. Only then is the module ready for data transactions. Skipping steps can leave pins nonfunctional or tristated.
Remarks: Encapsulate init in a single driver routine and gate subsequent API calls on a completed-init flag.

feature_name: Interrupt generation when configured as output (for debug)
description: GPIO pins configured as outputs can still generate interrupts when software toggles their state. This capability is useful to verify interrupt routing and ISR behavior without external stimulus. It leverages the same edge-detection logic synchronized to the GPIO clock. Direction setting does not inhibit interrupt path. This facilitates rapid bring-up and test.
dependencies: CPU interrupt controller; Edge trigger registers
Remarks: Use this to validate interrupt wiring before connecting external sources; remember to restore pin direction afterward.

description: GPIO operation is independent of processor endianness. Register semantics and bit positions are unaffected by byte ordering. This simplifies portable driver code across big- and little-endian system builds. No special handling is required in the GPIO block. Behavior remains consistent across endianness modes.
Remarks: Standard memory-mapped IO accessors are sufficient regardless of endianness settings.

description: The GPIO peripheral is not affected by emulation suspend events such as halts and breakpoints. Pin states and edge detection continue according to hardware when the CPU is halted. This can aid timing-sensitive debug where external signals must keep running. Developers should be aware of pending interrupts accumulating during emulation halts.
Remarks: Mask or service pending flags after halts to avoid burst ISR execution on resume.

description: INTSTAT01 uses write-1-to-clear behavior for per-pin pending bits. Reading shows a snapshot of pending edge events per bank. Writing a 1 clears the corresponding pending bit while a 0 has no effect. Software must clear bits after servicing to rearm edge detection properly. This mechanism avoids destructive read semantics.
Remarks: Read-modify-write is unnecessary; write the mask of serviced pins directly to INTSTAT01.

feature_name: Register topology and naming (XY, n fields)
description: Register names aggregate two banks per 32-bit register using a XY suffix, and field names use an n suffix matching the pin index. Bit positions 0–31 correspond to GP[0]–GP[31] across banks. This consistent topology simplifies generic driver loops and masks. The mapping table details bank and bit assignments. It standardizes control across banks and pins.
Remarks: Implement helpers that compute masks from pin numbers to target the correct XY fields.

description: Some GPIO pins may use open-drain output buffers permitting wired-logic connections. For such pins, IN_DATA reflects the resolved bus level which may differ from the device's drive intent. The presence and selection of open-drain pads are device/package specific and documented in the data manual. The GPIO block itself does not configure pad mode. Designs must include appropriate pull resistors.
Remarks: Validate pad type in the device DM before assuming push-pull drive; add pull-ups for open-drain nets.

feature_name: Bank-level interrupt enable affects EDMA too
description: The BINTEN register controls both CPU interrupt enabling and EDMA event enable per bank. Disabling a bank in BINTEN suppresses both types of events from its pins. Proper configuration ensures intended synchronization sources reach EDMA or CPU. This linkage is important when partitioning load between CPU ISRs and DMA handlers.
Remarks: Document BINTEN policy so DMA flows are not inadvertently starved by bank masking.

feature_name: Clock-domain synchronization of edges and inputs
description: Edge detection and input reads are synchronized to the GPIO clock domain. This prevents metastability but introduces one or more clock-cycle latency relative to external events. The clock is CPU/6 once enabled. Timing of interrupts and EDMA events follows this domain. Software should account for this when measuring latency.
Remarks: Budget ISR/EDMA trigger latency with the CPU/6 peripheral clock and synchronization pipeline in mind.

description: The GPIO peripheral itself is not compliant with any external bus standards. While software can implement protocols over GPIO, the block provides generic bit I/O only. There is no inherent standards negotiation or electrical compliance. This frames expectations for interoperability. External compliance must be ensured by higher-level software and board design.
Remarks: Use protocol-specific drivers on top of GPIO bit-banging where required by standards.
